content.full_text:
STATI·: OF CAI.JFOKNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
, 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(l) 16) !l :J-5(1611 • Fax ( 9 16) :1:.!!!•llHH h 
Robert Chavez 
Chief Executive Officer 
Urban Corps of San Diego County 
3127 Jefferson Street 
San Diego, CA 92110 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-239 
Dear Mr. Chavez: 
November 9,2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Urban Corps of San Diego 
County (the "Urban Corps") regarding Government Code Section 1090 et seq.1 We are only 
providing advice regarding Section I 090, and we offer no opinion on the applicability of other 
conflict of interest laws. We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (/11 re Oglesby ( 197 5) 
I FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to it to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County District 
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097. l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written 
response from either entity. (Section 1097.1 (c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for 
purposes of Section I 090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against 
any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 ( c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of San Diego from entering into a contract with the 
Urban Corps, a nonprofit ·entity under Section 501 ( c )(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, to provide 
landscape design-build services to implement the Martin Luther King, Jr. Way Streetscape 
Improvement Project given that the Urban Corps was extensively involved in the design of the 
Project and developed an initial design concept for the Project? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering into the contract with the Urban 
Corps because the Urban Corps is not subject to Section 1090 with respect to the Project based on 
the facts presented. 
1 All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-239 
Page No. 2 
Founded in 1989, the Urban Corps is a nonprofit entity under Section 501(c)(3) of the 
Internal Revenue Code, a certified Community Conservation Corps under the Public Resources 
Code, a charter school under the Education Code, and a licensed general contractor with an in­
house landscape architect and construction department. According to Urban Corp's website, its 
mission is "to provide young adults with a high school education combined with job training and 
community service in the fields of conservation and recycling, which assist youth in becoming more 
employable while protecting San Diego's natural resources and instilling the importance of 
community service." 
The Broadway Heights neighborhood is located within the community of Encanto, which is 
one of the 52 communities that make up the City of San Diego. In 2014, the Broadway Heights 
Community Council, a private organization of members who live within the Broadway Heights 
neighborhood, worked with the City to design streetscape improvements to Martin Luther King, Jr. 
Way, a roadway within the neighborhood (the "Martin Luther King, Jr. Way Streetscape 
Improvement Project"). However, the costs of the desired improvements substantially exceeded the 
City's budget for the Project. 
In 2016, the City contacted the Urban Corps for assistance with community outreach and 
design of the Project, to incorporate the community's recommendations while staying within the 
City's budget for the Project. The Urban Corps' landscape architect and other staff then began 
working with the community to revise and finalize the Project's design, including hosting 
stakeholder meetings. 
Through this work with the community, the Urban Corps has developed an initial design 
concept for the Project. The Urban Corps has submitted a proposal to the City to provide landscape 
design-build services for the Project. Under the proposal, the Urban Corps would donate its Project 
design costs, representing approximately 40 percent of total Project costs. The proposal would also 
create meaningful job training opportunities for the Urban Corps' charter school students and young 
adults enrolled in its job training program. 
ANALYSIS 
Section I 090 provides, in pertinent part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, 
district, judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any 
contract made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are 
members." 
Interpreting the term "employees" as used in Section I 090, the California Supreme Court 
recently affirmed the long-standing rule from case law that independent contractors are not 
categorically excluded from Section I 090: "Liability under the statute can extend to independent 
contractors who have duties to engage in or advise on public contracting."2 For example, a 
"corporate consultant" serving as an independent contractor for a state or local government agency 
that "has a hand in designing and developing the plans and specifications for the project" is subject 
2 People,. Superior Court (Sal,lofbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230,233. 


File No. A-17-239 
Page No. 3 
to Section 1090.3 However, no State appellate court has ever determined that a nonprofit entity 
fonned primarily to serve and promote public purposes is subsequently prohibited from a contract 
under Section I 090 because the government agency chose to consult the nonprofit in the design of a 
public project. 
The Urban Corps is a nonprofit entity under Section 50l(c)(3) of the United States Internal 
Revenue Code. Because the Urban Corps is a certified Community Conservation Corps under the 
Public Resources Code and a charter school under the Education Code, a strong inference exists 
that the State intended the Urban Corps to contract with public entities with regard to projects that 
fulfill the Urban Corps' dual purposes as a Community Conservation Corps and a charter school, 
such as the Martin Luther King, Jr. Way Streetscape Improvement Project. Given that inference, 
and that the Urban Corps would donate its design costs related to the Project under the proposal, the 
Urban Corps is not subject to Section 1090 with respect to the Project based on the facts presented. 
Accordingly, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering into a contract with the Urban 
Corps for the provision oflandscape design-build services relating to the Project.4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
By: 
-tv( 
Counsel, Legal Division 
MFC:jgl 
3 Davis v. Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300-301. 
4 We note that this conclusion should not be construed as a finding that nonprofit entities or officers of 
nonprofit entities are not subject to Section l 090 under other circumstances. The California Supreme Court recently 
held that liability under Section I 090 extends to independent contractors "who have duties to engage in or advise on 
public contracting" (Sahlolbei, supra, at p. 233), or "have been entrusted with 'transact[ing] on behalf of the 
Government'" (id. at p. 240, citing Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565,570).

sections.question:
Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of San Diego from entering into a contract with the 
Urban Corps, a nonprofit ·entity under Section 501 ( c )(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, to provide 
landscape design-build services to implement the Martin Luther King, Jr. Way Streetscape 
Improvement Project given that the Urban Corps was extensively involved in the design of the 
Project and developed an initial design concept for the Project?
sections.conclusion:
No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering into the contract with the Urban 
Corps because the Urban Corps is not subject to Section 1090 with respect to the Project based on 
the facts presented. 
1
